response.evidence: Hispanic or Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic counterparts across all racial and gender groups. For example: Asian Males (Hispanic: 79.9% vs Non-Hispanic: 87.8%), Asian Females (Hispanic: 77.5% vs Non-Hispanic: 86.5%), White Males (Hispanic: 79.2% vs Non-Hispanic: 84.5%), White Females (Hispanic: 75.9% vs Non-Hispanic: 83.8%), and Black Males (Hispanic: 64.8% vs Non-Hispanic: 70.5%). The pattern is systematic across all 10 comparisons, with Hispanic applicants experiencing 4-8 percentage point lower acceptance rates despite similar debt-to-income ratios (all hovering around 43-44%) across groups.

Across all racial groups, Hispanic or Latino applicants consistently show lower acceptance rates compared to their Non-Hispanic or Latino counterparts of the same race and sex. For example: White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (84.5%), Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (83.8%), and Black Female Hispanic (69.5%) vs Black Female Non-Hispanic (73.0%). This pattern holds across nearly all demographic combinations, with acceptance rate gaps ranging from 3.5 to 7.9 percentage points, suggesting systematic disadvantage for Hispanic or Latino applicants even when controlling for race and sex.

Hispanic or Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial and gender groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (84.5%); White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (83.8%); Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.8%); Asian Female Hispanic (77.5%) vs Asian Female Non-Hispanic (86.5%). This pattern holds across all demographic combinations, with Hispanic applicants showing 4-8 percentage point lower acceptance rates despite similar average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% range for both groups).

The data shows acceptance rates for Hispanic or Latino applicants range from 50.0% to 79.9% across different race/sex combinations. Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants show acceptance rates ranging from 70.5% to 87.8%. While some Hispanic groups have lower acceptance rates, others are comparable or higher than some non-Hispanic groups. For example, Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) exceeds White Male Hispanic (79.2%) and several non-Hispanic groups like Black or African American Male (70.5%). The variation in acceptance rates appears more strongly associated with race and sex combinations than ethnicity alone. Without controlling for other financial factors (loan amounts, income, debt ratios which vary across groups), the data does not provide clear evidence of systematic discrimination based solely on Hispanic or Latino ethnicity.

Hispanic or Latino applicants consistently show lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino counterparts across all racial and gender groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (84.5%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (83.8%), Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.8%), Asian Female Hispanic (77.5%) vs Asian Female Non-Hispanic (86.5%). This pattern holds across all demographic categories, with Hispanic applicants showing 4-8 percentage point lower acceptance rates despite similar financial profiles (comparable loan amounts, debt-to-income ratios, and loan-to-value ratios).

Hispanic or Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial and gender groups. For example: Asian Male Hispanic 79.9% vs Non-Hispanic 87.8%; Asian Female Hispanic 77.5% vs Non-Hispanic 86.5%; White Male Hispanic 79.2% vs Non-Hispanic 84.5%; White Female Hispanic 75.9% vs Non-Hispanic 83.8%; Black Male Hispanic 64.8% vs Non-Hispanic 70.5%; Black Female Hispanic 69.5% vs Non-Hispanic 73.0%. This 4-9 percentage point gap persists across all demographic combinations, suggesting ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval rates.

Hispanic or Latino applicants consistently show lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic counterparts across all racial groups. For example: White Hispanic males have 79.2% acceptance vs 84.5% for White non-Hispanic males; Asian Hispanic males have 79.9% vs 87.8% for non-Hispanic; Black Hispanic males have 64.8% vs 70.5% for non-Hispanic. This pattern holds across all demographic combinations, with Hispanic applicants having 5-8 percentage points lower acceptance rates despite similar average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups), suggesting ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval outcomes.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race and sex categories have acceptance rates ranging from 50.0% to 79.9%. Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 70.5% to 87.8%. While some Hispanic groups show lower acceptance rates, the variation appears largely driven by race rather than ethnicity alone. For example, among Asians, Hispanic acceptance rates are 77.5% (Female) and 79.9% (Male) compared to 86.5% and 87.8% for non-Hispanic - a moderate difference. Among Whites, Hispanic acceptance rates are 75.9% (Female) and 79.2% (Male) versus 83.8% and 84.5% for non-Hispanic - also moderate differences. The data suggests ethnicity interacts with race and sex, but does not provide conclusive evidence of systematic discrimination against Hispanic/Latino applicants as a whole, as many Hispanic groups show acceptance rates above 75%.

Hispanic or Latino applicants consistently show lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial and gender groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (84.5%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (83.8%), Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (87.8%), Asian Female Hispanic (77.5%) vs Non-Hispanic (86.5%), Black Male Hispanic (64.8%) vs Non-Hispanic (70.5%), Black Female Hispanic (69.5%) vs Non-Hispanic (73.0%). This pattern of 4-8 percentage point gaps persists across all demographic segments, suggesting systematic disparity in loan approval outcomes for Hispanic or Latino applicants.

Hispanic or Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial and gender groups. For example: Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (87.8%), Asian Female Hispanic (77.5%) vs Non-Hispanic (86.5%), White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs Non-Hispanic (84.5%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (83.8%), Black Male Hispanic (64.8%) vs Non-Hispanic (70.5%), Black Female Hispanic (69.5%) vs Non-Hispanic (73.0%). This pattern holds across all demographic combinations with acceptance rate gaps ranging from 4-8 percentage points, suggesting systematic disadvantage for Hispanic or Latino applicants even when controlling for race and gender.
